findings.title: Channel deps optional in constructor, checked at connect() runtime
findings.description: Encapsulation: 8/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 4/10 (Aggregate: 6.0). All three new channel classes (EmailChannel, FeishuChannel, DingTalkChannel) accept deps as optional constructor parameter, storing null if missing. The invariant 'a channel must have its dependencies to function' is deferred to connect() where it throws at runtime. This creates a period where the object exists in an invalid state: it has been constructed but cannot perform any useful work. A consumer calling sendMessage() gets a different error ('channel not connected') than the real problem ('deps missing'). Making deps required in the constructor would make the invalid state unrepresentable at construction time.
findings.suggestion: Make deps a required constructor parameter. If DI requires lazy creation, use a factory function that validates deps before constructing the channel: export function createEmailChannel(config: EmailConfig, deps: EmailChannelDeps): EmailChannel. This shifts the error from runtime connect() to construction time.

findings.title: Unsafe `as` cast on IMAP exists event data without type guard
findings.code_snippet: const existsData = data as { path: string, count: number, prevCount: number }
findings.description: Encapsulation: 8/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 4/10 (Aggregate: 6.0). The IMAP client 'exists' event handler receives `data: unknown` and immediately casts it with `as` to an assumed shape. If the IMAP library changes its event payload shape or sends unexpected data, this will silently produce undefined values used in arithmetic (line 169-170: `existsData.count - existsData.prevCount`), potentially causing NaN propagation or incorrect message fetching. Same pattern as previously flagged `as BaileysUpsertEvent` and `as SlackMessage` in Task 16.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime type guard: function isExistsData(d: unknown): d is { path: string, count: number, prevCount: number } { return typeof d === 'object' && d !== null && typeof (d as any).count === 'number' && typeof (d as any).prevCount === 'number'; }. Use it before accessing properties, log and return early if guard fails.

findings.title: Unsafe `as DingTalkBotMessage` cast on JSON.parse result
findings.description: Encapsulation: 8/10, Expression: 6/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 6.5). JSON.parse returns `any` which is cast to DingTalkBotMessage without validation. While the surrounding try/catch will catch JSON.parse failures, it will NOT catch a structurally wrong payload -- e.g. missing `text.content` or `conversationId` fields would lead to undefined values propagating into IncomingMessage and session webhook storage. The DingTalk Stream protocol could evolve or send different event types on this same topic.
findings.suggestion: Add a minimal runtime shape check after JSON.parse: verify typeof message.msgId === 'string' && typeof message.conversationId === 'string' && typeof message.text?.content === 'string' before proceeding. This follows the SmartTip/MemoryExtractor validation pattern already established in this codebase.

findings.title: IncomingMessage remains mutable despite fan-out to multiple handlers
findings.code_snippet:
export interface IncomingMessage {
  id: string
  platform: ChannelPlatform
  channelId: string
  senderId: string
  senderName?: string
  text: string
  timestamp: Date
  raw?: unknown
}
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 7/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 5.25). IncomingMessage is constructed in each channel and passed to multiple handlers sequentially (messageHandlers loop in all 3 new channels). Any handler could mutate the message object before the next handler sees it, leading to subtle cross-handler interference. This was previously flagged in Task 16 review and remains unfixed. With 3 new channel implementations all sharing this pattern, the blast radius has increased.
findings.suggestion: Add readonly to all fields: export interface IncomingMessage { readonly id: string; readonly platform: ChannelPlatform; ... }. Alternatively, freeze the object before dispatching: Object.freeze(incomingMessage).

findings.title: Config interfaces accept any values without validation at boundary
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 7/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 5.25). EmailConfig, FeishuConfig, and DingTalkConfig are accepted by channel constructors without any runtime validation. EmailConfig.imap.port could be negative or >65535, fromAddress could be empty string, FeishuConfig.appId could be empty. These are Imperative Shell boundary types receiving external configuration, where Valibot validation would be appropriate per project standards. The discriminated union on `platform` is well done but insufficient alone.
findings.suggestion: Define Valibot schemas for each config type and validate in a factory function or at the ChannelRegistry.register() boundary. Example: const EmailConfigSchema = v.object({ platform: v.literal('email'), imap: v.object({ host: v.pipe(v.string(), v.minLength(1)), port: v.pipe(v.number(), v.minValue(1), v.maxValue(65535)), ... }) }).

findings.title: Feishu JSON.parse cast on message content without structural check
findings.code_snippet: const parsed = JSON.parse(message.content) as { text?: string }
findings.description: Encapsulation: 8/10, Expression: 6/10, Usefulness: 7/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 6.5). The Feishu message content is JSON-parsed and cast to { text?: string }. While there is a try/catch for parse errors and a null check on parsed.text, the cast itself could mask non-text message types that parse successfully but have a different shape (e.g. image messages with { image_key: string }). The message_type field is available on line 24 but is not checked before parsing as text.
findings.suggestion: Check message.message_type === 'text' before attempting to parse as text content. This is more robust than relying on the cast: if (message.message_type !== 'text') return;. The message_type check at the FeishuMessageEvent level provides a proper discriminant.

findings.title: StatusChangeHandler type alias duplicated across all 3 channel files
findings.description: Encapsulation: N/A, Expression: 6/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: N/A (Aggregate: 6.0). The type alias `StatusChangeHandler = (status: ChannelStatus) => void` is independently defined in email/index.ts (line 42), feishu/index.ts (line 47), and dingtalk/index.ts (line 40). The onStatusChange() method and statusHandlers array are also structurally identical across all three. This duplication is a minor DRY concern but could diverge over time.
findings.suggestion: Export StatusChangeHandler from types.ts alongside the other shared types, or extract a base channel class / mixin that provides the status management behavior (setStatus, onStatusChange, statusHandlers). This would also reduce the repeated handler dispatch boilerplate.

positive_observations: ChannelConfig discriminated union properly extended with EmailConfig | FeishuConfig | DingTalkConfig -- each variant has a literal platform discriminant field, enabling exhaustive switch/if-else narrowing
ChannelPlatform literal union correctly extended in lockstep with config variants -- 5-value union matches 5 config interfaces
All three new channel classes follow the established encapsulation pattern: private fields, getter-based status, DI via constructor deps, implements Channel interface
FeishuConfig.receiveIdType is a proper 3-value literal union ('chat_id' | 'open_id' | 'user_id') instead of plain string -- best practice for API enum parameters
DingTalkChannel.SessionWebhookEntry is a well-modeled domain type capturing webhook URL + expiry, with proper expiry check in getSessionWebhook()
ChannelRegistry.getByPlatform() now returns spread copy with Readonly wrapper -- FIXED from Task 16's mutable reference leak (P1)
ChannelRegistry.register() now validates channel.platform matches config.platform -- FIXED from Task 16's missing validation (P1)
Error handling follows cause-chaining pattern: throw new Error('...', { cause: error }) throughout all three channels
